posts.title: We built this website with the same tools we sell
posts.description: How Roble Media Lab rebuilt its own website with practical AI. What we used, what broke first, and the one shortcut we refused to take.
posts.html:
This website is a Roble Media Lab project, and the client was us.

Before we ask any business to trust us with AI, automation, or a website, we think it is fair to show that we run the same tools on our own business, with a real deadline and a real budget. So this post is a build log. The honest version, including the parts that did not work the first time.

The short version: this site went from a single static landing page to a full multi-page website with booking, a working contact process, analytics, and the publishing system this post sits in, rebuilt in under two weeks using the same practical AI approach we bring to client work.

Why we built it ourselves instead of outsourcing it

We could have paid another studio to make a nice site. Plenty of businesses do, and there is nothing wrong with that. But the whole point of Roble Media Lab is helping growing businesses use practical AI and automation in a grounded way. Selling that without doing it on our own business first would be hollow.

So we treated our own website as a client project. Same discovery, same build process, same standards we apply to paid work. If these tools genuinely let a small team ship real systems quickly, we should be able to show it, not just say it.

What runs on this site today

In plain terms, here is what this site does right now:

  • A full multi-page website, built to load fast on real Kenyan connections.
  • A booking page where you can pick a time and get a calendar invite automatically.
  • A contact form that lands with a real person: spam-checked, stored safely, and answered within one business day.
  • A click-to-chat WhatsApp button, because in Kenya that is where a lot of first conversations actually happen.
  • Privacy-conscious analytics, so we can see what is working without spying on anyone.
  • The blog and publishing system this very post sits in.

The tools behind it were mostly ChatGPT for thinking through plans, Claude for building and fixing the actual site, Cloudflare for hosting, and GitHub for keeping versions safe. None of them do the thinking for you. They make the building fast. Deciding what is worth building, and in what order, is still the real work, and it is the part we spend the most time on with clients too.

The parts that did not work the first time

This is the section most build stories leave out, so it is the one we want to be most honest about.

The first solution was usually not the best solution. Our first booking setup used one tool, then moved to another that fit better. Our first contact form ran on a third-party service, then we replaced it with our own build that asks sharper questions and helps us understand a visitor's problem before we ever talk. That is not indecision. That is what improving a system on evidence looks like, and it is the same loop we run on client work.

Some problems took patience rather than cleverness. There was a stretch where an embedded form and the booking widget simply would not behave inside the page, and the fix came from working through it step by step until it did what it was supposed to. That experience is now built into how we estimate: the tools do not remove the problems. They make the problems solvable.

AI didn't replace the need to think. It replaced the need to know everything before starting.

That single idea shapes how we scope every project.

The one shortcut we refused to take

Here is the choice we are proudest of, and it cost nothing but honesty.

When we built the site, we set up the sections a normal agency site has. A place for testimonials. A place for client case studies. A "trusted by" strip. The structure is all there, ready.

They are empty. On purpose.

We could have written a few convincing quotes and nobody would have known. But this business is built on being straight with people about what technology can and cannot do, and that has to start with what we publish about ourselves. So those sections stay empty until they are true. The day a project is complete and the client is happy to be named, they fill up honestly. If we would fake a testimonial, why would you trust anything else we tell you?

What this means if you run a growing business

If you have been told that AI and modern web tooling are only for technical teams, this build says otherwise. What it takes is a clearly named problem, a small first scope, and the patience to improve when the first attempt is wrong. The tools have become good enough that the limiting factor is no longer engineering headcount. It is knowing what to point the tools at.

That judgement is where most of the value sits, and it is learnable. If you want the longer version of how we choose those opportunities, we wrote it up in a practical guide to AI for small businesses.

Judge the work directly

This site is a working example of our business websites service: the speed, the search structure, the contact process that reaches a person within a business day. You are reading the portfolio piece.

If that is the kind of partner you are looking for, see how we can help, book a short discovery call, or send us a message. No pressure, no jargon. Just an honest look at whether there is a sensible way we can help.

posts.title: AI for small businesses: a practical guide to getting real value
posts.description: A grounded, hype-free guide to using AI in a small business. How to find genuine opportunities, avoid costly mistakes, and adopt tools your team will actually use.
posts.html:
If you run a small business, you've probably heard two things about AI in the same week. That it's going to transform everything you do. And that you're already behind for not using it. Neither one helps much when you're also doing payroll, chasing an invoice, and answering customer messages on WhatsApp.

This guide is written for that reality. It isn't a list of trending tools, and it won't promise that AI will replace half your work by next month. It's a straightforward way to think about where AI actually helps a small business, how to try it without wasting money, and how to sidestep the mistakes that leave owners frustrated and out of pocket.

The problem: pressure without a plan

Most business owners we talk to aren't sceptical about AI. They can see it's useful. What they're missing is a way to tell the genuinely valuable uses apart from the noise.

So a few things tend to happen. Some owners buy a subscription to a well-marketed tool, use it keenly for two weeks, then quietly drop it because it never fit how the business really works. Others freeze. There are too many options and too many opinions, no obvious first move, so nothing changes. And some hand a vague instruction to a staff member, something like "start using AI for our content," with no structure at all, then wonder why the results are all over the place or quietly create new problems.

None of that comes from a lack of effort. It comes from starting with the tool instead of the problem.

Why this keeps happening

AI gets sold the way most technology gets sold. Feature first, and to everyone at once. A demo shows you the best possible output, not the messy middle where your business actually lives. The pricing page talks about what the tool can do, not what it will change for you. And because the field moves fast, there's this constant low hum telling you that you should already be doing this by now.

Small businesses feel that pressure harder than big ones. You don't have an innovation budget or a team whose whole job is to experiment. Every hour and every shilling you spend testing something is taken from serving customers. So a wrong turn actually costs you, which is exactly why a calm, deliberate approach is worth more here, not less.

Here's the encouraging part. The calm approach is also the one that works. You don't need to adopt everything. You need to adopt the few things that fit your business, and adopt them properly.

A practical framework: friction first, tools last

Instead of asking "which AI tool should we use," start somewhere more useful. Ask where your work keeps slowing down, breaking, or depending on one person's memory. Those are the spots where AI is most likely to earn its keep. This is the sequence we walk through.

Map where the friction actually is

Spend a week just noticing. The tasks that repeat, the information people keep hunting for, the moments where work stalls as it passes from one person to another. Writing the same kind of email over and over. Re-typing details from one system into another. Digging through old chats for an answer a customer is waiting on. Those patterns tell you far more than any list of popular products. You're looking for work that's repetitive, low on judgement, and happens often.

Pick one narrow use case

Don't try to fix everything at once. One focused workflow is easier to test, safer to change, and much clearer to measure. "Draft a first reply to common enquiries that a person then checks and sends" is a good starting point. "Automate our whole customer service" is not, at least not yet. Keeping the scope small is what turns a nice idea into something you can actually judge.

Keep a person responsible

Good AI supports someone's judgement. It doesn't hide it. For any workflow you bring in, decide who checks the output, who handles the odd cases the system won't understand, and who can switch it off if something goes wrong. That one habit prevents most of the quiet risks that come with automating work.

Measure what actually changed

Before you expand anything, ask what really improved. Did it save time? Did the quality hold up? Did your team use it, or work around it? Track the honest answer, not the excitement of a new toy. If the evidence is there, do more of it. If it isn't, you found out cheaply and can move on.

It's a deliberately unflashy process. It's also how AI stops being a distraction and starts being an advantage. If you want a longer look at just the first step, we wrote a companion piece on where a small business should start with AI.

What this looks like in practice

To make it concrete, here are a few examples. These show the kind of opportunity the framework tends to surface. They're illustrative, not results we're claiming on anyone's behalf.

Take a small service business getting dozens of similar first-contact messages a day. Rather than "automating support," it might use AI to draft a structured first response and pull together the details a human needs, while a real person still reviews and sends. The goal is a faster, steadier first reply with a person firmly in the loop.

Or picture a team copying the same client details between a booking form, a spreadsheet, and an invoice. That repetition is a friction point long before it's an "AI project." Sometimes the fix is a simple automation. Sometimes it's removing a step altogether. Mapping the workflow first is what tells you which one you're looking at. We go deeper on that in map the workflow before you automate it.

Then there's the owner who's the only person who knows how everything gets done. Here AI is less about speed and more about helping write down how the work actually happens, so the business leans less on one person's memory. The value is resilience, not a clever demo.

Notice that none of these start with a tool. They start with an ordinary, recognisable problem. That's the whole point.

Common mistakes to avoid

A handful of patterns show up again and again. Knowing them ahead of time saves real money.

Buying the tool before naming the problem. A subscription isn't a strategy. If you can't say exactly what friction a tool removes, you're not ready to pay for it.

Trying to automate everything at once. Big, ambitious automation is fragile and hard to trust. Small workflows you expand once they've proven themselves last a lot longer.

Taking the human out completely. It's tempting to remove people to save time, but on anything that needs judgement or touches a customer, that's usually where the errors and the reputation damage creep in.

Ignoring your data and privacy responsibilities. Feeding sensitive customer information into a tool without knowing where it goes is a real risk, not a hypothetical one. Handle client data the way you always would.

Chasing guarantees. Be careful with anyone promising rankings, virality, or "10x" results. You can control sound strategy and careful execution. You can't control every outcome, and honest partners say so.

Your practical next steps

If you want to go from reading to doing, here's a simple way to start:

  1. For one week, jot down every task that feels repetitive or slow.
  2. Pick the single most frequent, lowest-judgement one on that list.
  3. Decide what a good result looks like, and who owns checking it.
  4. Try one focused workflow for that task, with a person reviewing the output.
  5. After two weeks, be honest with yourself: keep it, adjust it, or drop it.

That's genuinely enough to begin. You don't need a big budget or a technical background. You need a clear problem and the discipline to start small.

Where we can help

At Roble Media Lab, this is exactly how we approach AI. Useful before impressive, honest about what it can and can't do, and built around the people who'll actually use it. Whether you're still deciding which opportunity is worth starting with or you already know the workflow you want to fix, AI and business automation covers the same ground this post does: rank the opportunities, build the one that earns its place, then make sure your team is confident enough to run it.

If the repetitive work is customer conversations rather than internal admin, WhatsApp automation is usually the more direct place to start. And if people can't find you in the first place, the bottleneck isn't AI at all, it's your website and the content around it.

If you'd like a grounded second opinion on where AI could genuinely help your business, the best first step is a short conversation. Book a 30-minute discovery call and we'll talk through the problem, your current workflow, and whether there's a sensible way to help. No pressure, no hype.

posts.title: Where should a small business start with AI?
posts.description: A grounded framework for finding useful AI opportunities without buying tools first.
posts.html:
Begin with friction, not software

Ask where your team repeats the same low-judgement task, searches for the same information, or loses time moving work between people. Those patterns are more useful than a list of popular AI products.

Choose a narrow first use case

A focused workflow is easier to test, safer to change, and clearer to measure. Examples might include turning meeting notes into structured actions or drafting a first response that a person reviews.

Keep a person responsible

AI output needs ownership. Decide who checks accuracy, handles unusual situations, and can turn the system off. Useful automation supports judgement rather than hiding it.

Measure the real change

Track time saved, error rates, adoption, and the quality of the handoff, not the novelty of the tool. Expand only when the evidence supports it.

Once you have picked a candidate workflow, map it before you automate it. Turning that map into something dependable is what AI and business automation is for, and the longer version of this framework is in our practical guide to AI for small businesses.

posts.title: Your business needs a content system, not just a calendar
posts.description: Why consistent content depends on decisions, roles, templates, and feedback, not a spreadsheet alone.
posts.html:
A calendar only records decisions

Consistency comes from knowing who the content is for, which business goals it supports, who owns each step, and how learning returns to the plan. A publishing calendar is helpful, but it cannot make those decisions for you.

Build the layers beneath it

Define a small set of audience problems and content pillars. Create repeatable formats and clear review rules. Then choose a planning rhythm your team can sustain.

Make feedback part of the system

A useful content system includes a simple way to review audience response, sales conversations, and team effort. The plan should become better informed over time, not merely fuller.

Building those layers is what SEO and content strategy covers: the pillars, the templates, and the publishing workflow, tied to what people are actually searching for. If the content is landing but the website it points to makes enquiring hard, that's the more urgent fix.

posts.title: Map the workflow before you automate it
posts.description: How a simple process audit prevents expensive, fragile automations.
posts.html:
Document reality first

Write down what triggers the process, which information is required, who makes decisions, what can go wrong, and what a successful result looks like. The awkward exceptions are often more important than the happy path.

Remove before you automate

Some steps exist only because an old process required them. Simplify, combine, or remove unnecessary work before introducing automation.

Design the handoff

Every dependable automation needs clear ownership, useful logging, and a way for a person to intervene. That is what turns a clever demo into an operating system a business can trust.

Mapping the workflow first is the whole basis of how we approach AI and business automation. If you are still deciding which process deserves the attention, where to start with AI covers how to choose one.
